BCC125_sea_lamprey_scRNA

Stephanie Hickey

2026-06-22

Lab: Weiming Li

Project: BCC125_sea_lamprey_scRNA

Sample: TM_C00004374

library(tidyverse)
library(Matrix)
library(SingleCellExperiment)
library(monocle3)
library(DropletUtils)
library(scater)
library(ape)
source(paste0(params$project_dir, "/src/scRNA_seq_functions.R"))
results_dir = paste0(params$project_dir, "/results/TM_QC")
# results_dir = "/mnt/ufs18/rs-013/bioinformaticsCore/projects/chong_davidson/BCC125_sea_lamprey_scRNA/results/TM_QC"
data_dir = paste0(params$project_dir, "/data")
# data_dir = "/mnt/ufs18/rs-013/bioinformaticsCore/projects/chong_davidson/BCC125_sea_lamprey_scRNA/data"
sampleID = params$sampleID
mat = readMM(paste0(data_dir, "/", sampleID, "/raw_matrix/matrix.mtx.gz"))
barcodes = read.delim(paste0(data_dir, "/", sampleID, "/raw_matrix/barcodes.tsv.gz"), header=F)
barcodes$V1 = paste0(sampleID, "_", barcodes$V1)

genes = read.delim(paste0(data_dir,"/", sampleID, "/raw_matrix/features.tsv.gz"), header=F)

rownames(mat) = genes$V1
colnames(mat) = barcodes$V1
sce <- SingleCellExperiment(assays = list(counts = mat))

Detect empty droplets with DropletUtils

# Set a seed. This can be any number. Using the same number every time ensures that a sequence of random numbers generated is the same every time you run your code.
set.seed(100)

# set `by.rank` to the max number of cells you expect
e.out <- emptyDrops(counts(sce), 
                    by.rank = as.numeric(params$expected_cells)) 

# add barcodes to a column
e.out$barcode = rownames(e.out)

# make a vector of significant barcodes
# use a false discovery rate (FDR) of 0.1%,
# meaning that no more than 0.1% of our called 
# barcodes should be empty droplets on average.
sig_barcodes <- 
  as_tibble(e.out) %>%
  filter(FDR < .001) %>% 
  pull(barcode)

Empty droplets often contain RNA from the ambient solution, resulting in non-zero counts after debarcoding. The emptyDrops function is designed to distinguish between empty droplets and cells. It does so by testing each barcode’s expression profile for significant deviation from the ambient profile. Using this method, 18438 barcodes represent cells.

Below is a barcode rank plot, or knee plot, which shows the (log-)total UMI count for each barcode on the y-axis and the (log-)rank on the x-axis. The plot resembles a curve with a sharp “knee” or “elbow.” Cells with high RNA content (likely real biological cells), are on the left side of the plot. Low-RNA-content cells (likely empty droplets or low-quality cells) are on the right side. The “knee” or transition point marks the threshold between high-quality cells and low-quality or empty droplets.

br.out <- barcodeRanks(counts(sce))

# make the rownames a column
br.out$barcodes <- rownames(br.out)

br.df =
  as.data.frame(br.out) %>%
  mutate(keep_cells = case_when(
   barcodes %in% sig_barcodes ~ "TRUE",
   !barcodes %in% sig_barcodes ~ "FALSE"
  ))

# Making the knee plot.
ggplot(br.df, aes(x=rank, y=total)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = keep_cells)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("black","red")) +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=metadata(br.out)$knee,
             linetype="knee"),
             color = "dodgerblue") +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=metadata(br.out)$inflection,
             linetype="inflection"),
             color = "forestgreen") +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = "legend", 
                        values = c(2, 2), 
                        guide = guide_legend(override.aes = list(color = c("forestgreen", "dodgerblue")))) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans='log10') +
  scale_y_continuous(trans='log10') +
  xlab("Rank") +
  ylab("Total Counts")

ggsave(file = paste0(results_dir,"/", sampleID, "_kneeplot.png"))
 sce <- sce[,sig_barcodes]

Identifying low quality cells

Common metrics include for identifying low quality cells include:

  • Total counts
  • Number of expressed features (genes)
  • Proportion of reads mapped to genes in the mitochondrial genome

For each cell, we calculate these QC metrics using the perCellQCMetrics() function from the scater package.

# Identifying the mitochondrial transcripts in our SingleCellExperiment.

# mouse mito genes start with "mt"
# human is "MT"
# change for different species
# this is a numeric vector of row numbers
# corresponding to mito genes
# NC_001626.1
all_genes = read.delim("/mnt/research/bioinformaticsCore/shared/Genomes/Petromyzon_marinus/GCF_010993605.1_kPetMar1.pri_genes.txt", header=F)

mito_genes = 
  all_genes %>% 
  filter(V1 == "NC_001626.1") %>%
  pull(V6)

mito_genes = gsub("gene ", "", mito_genes)
is.mito = which(rownames(sce) %in% mito_genes)

# get the per cell stats
stats <- perCellQCMetrics(sce,
                          subsets=list(Mito=is.mito))

Summarize the stats

The total counts in each cell:

summary(stats$sum)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    7500    8907   10514   12109   13654  101988

The number of genes with at least one count in each cell:

summary(stats$detected)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##     426    1919    2140    2267    2499    7516

These are the genes on the mitochondrial chromosome, NC_001626.1.

mito_genes
##  [1] "CYTB" "ND1"  "ND2"  "COX1" "COX2" "ATP8" "ATP6" "COX3" "ND3"  "ND4L"
## [11] "ND4"  "ND5"  "ND6"

The percent of counts that come from mitochondria genes:

summary(stats$subsets_Mito_percent)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
## 0.01482 1.85769 2.19103 2.19017 2.54750 6.34276

Filter cells with high mitochondria gene reads

high.mito <- isOutlier(stats$subsets_Mito_percent, type="higher")

I propose removing every cell with percent mito > 3.7246424, which is 3 median absolute deviations (MAD) above the median percent mito. This is 114 out of 18438 cells.

counts = counts(sce)
cds <- new_cell_data_set(counts,
                         cell_metadata = stats)
colData(cds)$discard = high.mito
saveRDS(cds, file = paste0(data_dir, "/", sampleID, "_cds.Rds"))

Cluster cells

colData(cds)$barcode = rownames(colData(cds))
colData_df = as.data.frame(colData(cds))

keep_cells =
  colData_df %>%
  filter(discard == FALSE) %>%
  pull(barcode)

cds = cds[,keep_cells]
cds <- preprocess_cds(cds, num_dim = 100)
cds <- reduce_dimension(cds)
cds <- cluster_cells(cds, resolution=1e-5)
saveRDS(cds, file = paste0(data_dir, "/", sampleID, "_cds_mito_filter.Rds"))

plot_cells(cds)
ggsave(file = paste0(results_dir, "/", sampleID, "_cluster_mito_filtered_UMAP.png"))
cds <- readRDS(paste0(data_dir, "/", sampleID, "/", sampleID, "_cds_mito_filter.Rds"))
plot_cells(cds)

Check for enrichment of known cell-type markers

This table contains the top ~100 cluster enriched genes for each cluster after removing mitochondria genes. It is saved in TM_C00004374_cluster_marker_genes_mito_filtered.csv. The markers can help us map a cluster to a cell type.

Column descriptions

  • gene_id vector of gene names
  • cell_group character vector of the cell group to which the cell belongs
  • marker_score numeric vector of marker scores as the fraction expressing scaled by the specificity. The value ranges from 0 to 1.
  • mean_expression numeric vector of mean normalized expression of the gene in the cell group
  • fraction_expressing numeric vector of fraction of cells expressing the gene within the cell group
  • specificity numeric vector of a measure of how specific the gene’s expression is to the cell group based on the Jensen-Shannon divergence. The value ranges from 0 to 1.
  • pseudo_R2 numeric vector of pseudo R-squared values, a measure of how well the gene expression model fits the categorical data relative to the null model. The value ranges from 0 to 1.
  • marker_test_p_value numeric vector of likelihood ratio p-values
  • marker_test_q_value numeric vector of likelihood ratio q-values
marker_test_res <- top_markers(cds,
                               genes_to_test_per_group = 100,
                               group_cells_by="cluster", cores=8)

write.csv(marker_test_res, file = paste0(results_dir, "/", sampleID, "_cluster_marker_genes_mito_filtered.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
library(DT)
datatable(marker_test_res, filter = "top")

We’ll check for the significance of the overlap between the top 100 cluster enriched genes and the supplied known cell-type markers. In the plot below, the rows represent cell types with know marker genes, and the columns represent the cell clusters. The number in each box is the number of genes shared between the top 100 cluster enriched genes for the specified clusters and the specified known cell-type markers. Each box is colored by the significance of the overlap (\(-log10(FDR)\)).

# load known marker genes
library(readr)
chung_davidson_cell_type_markers <- read_csv(paste0(data_dir, "/chung_davidson_cell_type_markers.csv"))
chung_davidson_cell_type_markers = 
  chung_davidson_cell_type_markers %>%
  select(-GeneName)

marker_test_genes = 
  marker_test_res %>%
  select(gene_id, cell_group)

cluster_ol =  phyperOverlapSets(chung_davidson_cell_type_markers, 
                                marker_test_genes, nrow(genes))

write.csv(cluster_ol$intersecting.genes, file = paste0(results_dir, "/", sampleID, "_marker_gene_overlaps_mito_filtered.csv"), row.names = FALSE) 

scores = cluster_ol$pval.scores

scores = 
  scores %>%
  rename(Group1 = "CellTypes",
         Group2 = "Clusters") %>%
  group_by(CellTypes) %>%
    mutate(FDR = p.adjust(Pval, "BH"))

scores$FDR[scores$FDR > .01] = 1

plot1 =
    ggplot(scores,
           aes(Clusters, CellTypes)) + 
           geom_tile(aes(fill = -log10(FDR)), 
                     color = "light gray") +
        #Creating legend
        guides(fill=guide_colorbar(title = "-log10(FDR)",
                                   barheight = 10,
                                   barwidth = 2,
                                   default.unit = "mm")) +
        #Creating color range
        scale_fill_gradientn(colors=c("white",
                                      "#fd9668",
                                      "#f1605d",
                                      "#de4968",
                                      "#9e2f7f"),
                             guide="colorbar",
                             limits = c(0, 23)) +
        #Rotating labels
        theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, 
                                         hjust = 0,vjust=-0.05),
              ) +
        theme(text = element_text(size = 7),
              axis.text = element_text(size = 7)) +
        geom_text(aes(label = nSharedGenes), size = 1) +
        coord_fixed()

plot1

ggsave(plot1, file = paste0(results_dir, "/", sampleID, "_overlap_heatmap_mito_filtered.png"))

A table with the overlapping genes is shown below. Filtering for Group1 = “Male Germ Cells” and Group2 = “1” will show the genes shared between those groups.

datatable(cluster_ol$intersecting.genes, filter = "top", rownames = FALSE)
